eli3: Bayes' theorem tells us how to update beliefs: P(cause|evidence) = P(evidence|cause) × P(cause) / P(evidence). It inverts conditional probabilities, going from 'if disease then symptom' to 'if symptom then disease'.

P(cause) is your prior belief before seeing evidence. P(evidence|cause) is the likelihood — how likely the evidence is if the cause is true. Multiply them, normalize, and you get the posterior P(cause|evidence).

This is the foundation of Bayesian statistics. Instead of just testing hypotheses, you maintain probability distributions over hypotheses and update them as data arrives. It's how spam filters, medical diagnosis, and many ML systems work.

whenWhy.title: Medical diagnosis
whenWhy.body: P(Disease|Symptoms) = P(Symptoms|Disease)P(Disease)/P(Symptoms). Even with highly accurate tests, rare diseases mean many false positives. Bayes' theorem correctly incorporates base rates to compute true probability.

whenWhy.title: Spam filtering
whenWhy.body: Naive Bayes classifiers compute P(Spam|Words) using Bayes' theorem with word independence assumption. Despite simplicity, highly effective for spam detection and text classification.

whenWhy.title: Bayesian machine learning
whenWhy.body: P(parameters|data) ∝ P(data|parameters)P(parameters). Prior over parameters updated by likelihood to give posterior. Enables uncertainty quantification, model comparison via Bayes factors, and automatic Occam's razor.

whenWhy.title: A/B testing and Bayesian optimization
whenWhy.body: Maintain posterior over conversion rates. Update as data arrives. Bayesian optimization uses posterior to guide exploration vs exploitation. Thompson sampling pulls arms proportional to probability of being best.

deepDiveIntro: Bayes' theorem is derived directly from the definition of conditional probability: P(A and B) can be written in two ways, and setting them equal and dividing gives the theorem. The key power is inversion — it lets you go from P(evidence given cause) to P(cause given evidence), which is usually what you actually want to know.

In Bayesian inference, the theorem is applied with parameters as the unknown and data as the evidence. The posterior belief about the parameters is proportional to how likely the data is under those parameters (the likelihood) times your prior belief. The denominator — the total probability of the data — normalises everything so the posterior sums to one; for conjugate priors, this normalisation has a closed-form solution.

deepDive.heading: The Theorem
deepDive.body: The theorem flips the direction of a conditional probability. To get P(A given B), multiply P(B given A) by the prior probability of A, then divide by the total probability of B. The denominator can be expanded using the law of total probability — sum P(B given each possible cause) weighted by the prior of each cause.

deepDive.heading: Bayesian Inference Framework
deepDive.body: The prior captures what you believe about the parameters before seeing any data. The likelihood measures how well each possible parameter value explains the observed data. Multiply them and normalise to get the posterior — your updated belief after seeing the data. Because the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior, each new batch of data can update the current posterior, which then becomes the prior for the next update.

deepDive.heading: Conjugate Priors
deepDive.body: A conjugate prior is one where the posterior has the same distributional family as the prior, just with updated parameters. For example, if you start with a Beta prior over a probability and observe coin flips (Binomial likelihood), the posterior is another Beta with the counts of heads and tails added to the original parameters. This makes Bayesian updating exact and computationally trivial — no numerical integration needed.
deepDive.formula: θ ~ Beta(α,β), X|θ ~ Bin(n,θ) ⟹ θ|X ~ Beta(α+X, β+n-X)

deepDive.heading: Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)
deepDive.body: Rather than working with the full posterior distribution, MAP gives you a single point estimate: the parameter value that maximises the posterior. This is like maximum likelihood estimation but with a prior acting as a regulariser. A Gaussian prior on the parameters corresponds to L2 (Ridge) regularisation, and a Laplace prior corresponds to L1 (Lasso) regularisation.

deepDive.heading: Marginal Likelihood and Model Selection
deepDive.body: The marginal likelihood (also called the evidence) is the probability of the data averaged over all parameter values under a given model. Comparing the evidence between two models gives the Bayes factor — a ratio that tells you how strongly the data favours one model over another. Complex models with many free parameters are automatically penalised because their prior probability mass is spread more thinly, providing a built-in Occam's razor.

takeaways: Conjugate priors give closed-form posterior updates.
MAP estimate adds prior regularization to MLE.
Marginal likelihood enables Bayesian model comparison.

edgeCases.q: What happens with an improper prior?
edgeCases.a: Improper priors don't integrate to 1 (e.g., P(θ) ∝ 1 over ℝ). Can still yield proper posteriors if likelihood is sufficiently concentrated. Jeffreys prior is a common improper prior invariant to reparameterization. Caution: improper priors can lead to improper posteriors or paradoxes in multi-parameter settings.

edgeCases.q: Why is the denominator P(B) hard to compute?
edgeCases.a: For continuous parameters, P(D) = ∫ P(D|θ)P(θ)dθ requires integrating over all parameter space — often high-dimensional and intractable. This is why MCMC (sampling) and variational inference (approximation) are essential for practical Bayesian computation.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between frequentist and Bayesian?
edgeCases.a: Frequentist: parameters are fixed, data is random. Confidence intervals and p-values describe data behavior under repeated sampling. Bayesian: parameters are random variables with distributions. Credible intervals describe uncertainty about parameters given observed data. Philosophically different; practically often similar with flat priors.

sources.label: Gelman et al. — Bayesian Data Analysis (3rd ed.)
MacKay — Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms
